verdict_summary: Situational; works for simple generated changelogs/backfills, prefer more active tools for new automated release workflows.
Situational; correctly classified as not a changelog tool.
Strong recommendation as a release orchestrator (not a changelog tool).
Works well in its intended context: multi-crate Gitoxide-style workspaces publishing to crates.io.
Narrow fit, use with clear expectations.
Unmaintained; does not install out of the box. Use git-cliff instead.
Recommended; no reservations for single-package npm projects.
Do not start here; works but deprecated and buggy on breaking changes.
Legacy; use only for existing installations. New projects: prefer git-cliff.
Recommended; the default Rust-era changelog generator to evaluate first.
Recommended, with a v6 migration warning.
Recommended for Go release pipelines; not recommended as a changelog-only tool.
Recommended with caveats; strongest fit for KAC-native workflows with tasks, fragments, validation, and release automation.
Situational; strong KAC verification and release helpers, but format conventions and repo migration details need attention.
Appears unmaintained for modern toolchains; does not currently work as tested.
Wooga-internal tooling; does not work out of the box and has no local use case.
Conditionally recommended; best for IntelliJ plugin projects.
Avoid; deprecated. Use glab release plus a generator instead.
Recommended for crates.io + GitHub projects; CI-native with limited local utility.
Situational (confirmed, narrower fit than the original article suggested).
Recommended, with a sharp scope caveat.
Works correctly but unmaintained; prefer the maintained drop-in (commit-and-tag-version) for new projects.
